title: What is GDP, really?
teachingGoal: Take a curious reader with no economics from 'I've heard the term' to genuinely understanding GDP: what it is, where the idea came from, how it reached India, how India actually computes it, what it tells you, what it cannot tell you, and the live argument over whether India even measures it correctly. They should finish able to read a GDP headline critically and explain it to someone else.
sectionArc: So what is GDP, really? (the one-breath answer, then final/domestic/flow, grounded in Indian production)
Where did this number even come from? (a 20th-century invention: Kuznets, the Depression and the war; his own welfare warning)
How did GDP reach India? (Naoroji's drain estimates, then the 1949 National Income Committee and the National Accounts Statistics)
How does India actually calculate it? (the three approaches; the production approach with corporate data; why the huge informal sector has to be estimated, not measured)
What is GDP telling you? (the size and growth of production; the rupee climb; per person)
What is GDP NOT telling you? (who got the money, unpaid work, the environment, informality; bullets earn their place here)
Did India even measure its growth right? (the Subramanian/PIIE critique, the mechanisms, the government rebuttal, the Feb 2026 revision; present all sides, cite, pick no winner)
mustTeach: GDP = total market value of final goods and services produced inside a country in a year
'final' and 'domestic' and 'a flow per year' are the load-bearing ideas
GDP measures production, not wealth, not income people receive, not wellbeing
India computes GDP three ways (output, income, expenditure) and the informal sector is estimated
the headline says nothing about distribution, unpaid work, or environmental cost
measuring GDP is genuinely contested: the formal-sector-as-proxy and deflator problems are real and debated

contextCards.fact: GDP and national income accounting are 20th-century inventions, built in the 1930s and 1940s to manage the Great Depression and finance the Second World War. The American economist Simon Kuznets developed the early US national-income estimates, and warned that the welfare of a nation can scarcely be inferred from a measure of national income.
contextCards.source: Kuznets, report to the US Congress, 1934 (standard economic history)

contextCards.fact: India's first official national-income estimates came from the National Income Committee, set up in 1949 and chaired by P. C. Mahalanobis, with V. K. R. V. Rao and D. R. Gadgil; it reported in the early 1950s and seeded today's National Accounts Statistics, compiled by the National Statistical Office under MoSPI. Decades earlier, in the 1860s and 1870s, Dadabhai Naoroji had made pioneering private estimates of India's per-capita income to argue that British rule was draining wealth from India.
contextCards.source: National Income Committee (Govt of India, 1949); standard Indian economic history

contextCards.fact: GDP can be built three equivalent ways: adding up value added by producers (output), adding up everyone's incomes, or adding up all spending. A hard part for India specifically is that a large share of activity is informal and unregistered, so the national accounts estimate it rather than measure it directly, often by using formal-sector or corporate data (such as the MCA21 company database) and labour-input methods as proxies.

Where GDP draws its 'production boundary' is a convention, not a law of nature. A meal cooked and sold in a restaurant counts; the same meal cooked at home does not. Feminist economists, notably Marilyn Waring, showed this leaves most of women's unpaid care and domestic work invisible in the headline number.
contextCards.source: Marilyn Waring, 'If Women Counted' (1988); SNA production boundary

contextCards.fact: Whether India even measures its growth correctly is a live, public argument. In a March 2026 working paper (Peterson Institute for International Economics, Working Paper 26-3, 'India's 20 Years of GDP Misestimation: New Evidence'), economists Abhishek Anand, Josh Felman and Arvind Subramanian argued India's growth was likely underestimated by about 1 to 1.5 percentage points a year in the 2005-2011 boom and overestimated by about 1.5 to 2 percentage points a year from 2012 to 2023. They blame two things: using the formal sector as a proxy for the much larger informal sector, which was hit hard after 2015 by demonetisation, the GST and COVID; and sector deflators tied to commodity prices that moved sharply. An earlier 2019 paper by Subramanian had put the overestimation at roughly 2.5 percentage points for 2011-2017.
contextCards.source: Anand, Felman & Subramanian, PIIE Working Paper 26-3, March 2026 (https://www.piie.com/publications/working-papers/2026/indias-20-years-gdp-misestimation-new-evidence); Subramanian, PIIE, 2019

contextCards.fact: The government has contested these claims. After Subramanian's 2019 paper, the Prime Minister's Economic Advisory Council issued a point-by-point rebuttal, calling the cross-country-regression method unusual and the conclusions unsupported, and noting his analysis leaned on private data and downplayed services and agriculture. Officials have also pointed out that India revised its GDP methodology again in February 2026, partly to address measurement concerns of exactly this kind. The debate is unsettled.
contextCards.source: PM Economic Advisory Council response (2019, PIB / press reporting); MoSPI methodology revision, February 2026
